ground_truth.title: Plumbing Battle Royale: Copper vs. CPVC vs. PEX Tubing
ground_truth.author: Brian Bohannan
ground_truth.main_content:
Piping options are like wrenches: plenty will get the job done, but every experienced plumber has their favorite for a reason. When comparing copper vs. PVC vs. PEX tubing, the debate often comes down to key factors like ease of installation, life expectancy, and cost. Each material has distinct strengths, making the choice highly dependent on your project's needs.

Understanding the nuances of piping materials — like the durability of copper pipe, the chemical resistance of CPVC, and the flexibility of PEX — empowers contractors to make informed decisions for technicians and homeowners alike. Whether you prioritize affordability or long-term reliability, today's copper vs. PVC vs. PEX showdown will help pick the right fit for any job.

Every professional plumber has encountered the copper vs. PVC vs. PEX debate at least once in their career, but chances are, you repeat this dilemma with every plumbing project. It often pops up when repairing residential plumbing systems in older homes with galvanized piping. These zinc-coated metal pipes corrode as they age, restricting water flow and releasing hazardous chemicals into the drinking water.

Copper plumbing pipes have been the golden choice for water lines since the 1930s, and for good reason: they are extremely durable and can tolerate extreme temperatures. With a melt point of 1981°F, copper tube is fire-resistant and earthquake-tolerant. Copper installations are also widely accepted by all building codes and do not support bacteria growth, making them ideal for large-scale water projects.

CPVC pipes include chlorine, making them safe for potable water and more durable over time. CPVC is a more inexpensive type of piping option compared to copper, plus, it can be installed much faster. It's more flexible and lightweight than its metal counterparts but still maintains a high resiliency to heat and high water pressure.

The last challenger in the copper vs. PVC vs. PEX debate is cross-linked polyethylene, better known as PEX pipe. As the newest plumbing material on the scene, PEX is a more affordable option that provides several advantages over metal pipe, including that it can handle extreme temperatures. It's a popular choice for retrofits, as it's highly flexible and can be snaked through walls and floors.

So, what material wins in the battle between copper vs. PVC vs. PEX? When long-term durability and fire resistance are the key to a project, choose copper pipe. CPVC is a more budget-friendly option with a decent life span, balancing affordability with chemical resistance. PEX is the number one pick for the cost factor and flexibility during installation, despite UV exposure limitations.
with: Piping options are like wrenches: plenty will get the job done, but every experienced plumber has their favorite for a reason.
Copper plumbing pipes have been the golden choice for water lines since the 1930s, and for good reason: they are extremely durable and can tolerate extreme temperatures.
Chlorinated polyvinyl chloride (CPVC) pipes were invented. CPVC pipes include chlorine, making them safe for potable water and more durable over time.
The last challenger in the copper vs. PVC vs. PEX debate is cross-linked polyethylene, better known as PEX pipe.
When long-term durability and fire resistance are the key to a project, choose copper pipe.
